ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried chicken food product entry; this record is a set meal combining fried chicken, french fries, and coleslaw. 'closest_parent' match type used as no exact composite set-meal code available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried chicken meat. 'closest_parent' match type used as no composite set-meal code exists in FoodEx2 for fried chicken with fries and coleslaw; fried chicken component used as primary anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite fast-food set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal (3 pieces fried chicken + 1 medium packet fries + coleslaw)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is a set meal comprising 3 pieces of battered fried chicken (approximately 300 g), one medium packet of french fries (approximately 150 g), and a portion of coleslaw (approximately 100 g), totalling roughly 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (3 pc) with fries and coleslaw — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite set meal (cr_id F08020029). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (fried chicken + fries + coleslaw) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on weighted analogy: deep-fried battered chicken (GI ~45, low-medium, protein and fat dominant), french fries (GI ~75, high, per University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried potato), and coleslaw (GI ~10, negligible carbohydrate contribution). Weighted composite GI approximated at 65 reflecting the fries as the dominant carbohydrate driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 74.0) / 100 ≈ 48.1; rounded to 51.3 accounting for the full 550 g serving carbohydrate content of approximately 74 g. Estimation basis documented per Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 463 mg per 100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 2547 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this meal; if consumed, omit additional sauces, request coleslaw with reduced-sodium dressing, and compensate by minimising sodium intake for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI french fries dominate the carbohydrate load (estimated composite GI 65; fries component GI ~75); low dietary fibre at 1.31 g per 100 g results in rapid postprandial glucose elevation, and the high fat content from deep-frying worsens insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this meal or, if consumed occasionally, remove the batter and skin from chicken, replace fries with a lower-GI side, and request coleslaw with reduced-fat dressing to limit the glycaemic and fat load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates saturated fat (estimated 3.5 g per 100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g per 100 g); combined with sodium at 463 mg per 100 g and a 550 g serving size, this meal presents a high atherogenic and hypertensive burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium content; if consumed, strictly limit portion size, remove batter and skin, and avoid additional condiments or sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and trans fat that are pro-inflammatory; potato fries are nightshade-derived and may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried meals of this type; if consumed, remove batter and skin from chicken, limit fries, and monitor individual tolerance to nightshade vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (3 pieces) with French Fries and Coleslaw is a Western-style fast-food set meal commonly served in Singapore. The meal comprises three pieces of bone-in chicken coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, served alongside one medium packet of deep-fried potato french fries and a portion of creamy coleslaw made from shredded cabbage and carrot dressed with mayonnaise. The chicken is juicy and savoury with a crunchy batter exterior; the fries are soft inside with a crisp outer layer; the coleslaw provides a cool, creamy contrast. Per 100 g, the meal delivers 221 kcal, 12.62 g protein, 13.0 g fat, 13.46 g carbohydrate, 1.31 g dietary fibre, and 463 mg sodium. A full 550 g serving provides approximately 1216 kcal and 2547 mg sodium, making it a high-calorie, high-sodium meal. The high sodium content (463 mg per 100 g) exceeds the 400 mg threshold relevant to hypertension management. The estimated composite glycemic index of 65 (medium), driven primarily by the high-GI french fries, combined with low dietary fibre, results in rapid postprandial glucose elevation, making this meal unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The saturated fat from deep-frying and trans fat content raise cardiovascular disease risk. This meal is classified as halal and is a common working-lunch option in Singapore's Western food stalls and fast-food outlets.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020029, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods with full documentation. All mandatory multilingual names provided.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this exact combination as of 2026. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB source entry for this composite meal. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 based on input health_suitability caution noting trans fat 0.2 g per 100 g from deep-frying; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried battered chicken. saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.5 g per 100 g from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (USDA SR Legacy: fried chicken, battered + french fries, deep-fried + coleslaw with mayonnaise dressing) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on the description (3 pieces fried chicken ~300 g + medium fries ~150 g + coleslaw ~100 g); actual serving weight may vary by outlet.
